[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又数载 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after several years.'
  • 生亡 - Concise phrase meaning 'the husband died.'
  • 披麻哭泣 - Euphemism for wearing mourning clothes and weeping.
  • 稽首 - Formal bow, a sign of deep respect or submission.
  • 新妇之责也 - Phrase meaning 'it is the duty of the new wife.'
  • 留一巨布囊 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'left a large cloth bag.'
  • 探此可得 - Phrase meaning 'by exploring this, one can obtain.'
  • 果不爽 - Phrase meaning 'indeed, it did not fail.'
  • 仰给此囊 - Phrase meaning 'rely on this bag for support.'
  • 家稍裕 - Phrase meaning 'the family became somewhat prosperous.'
  • 去如黄鹤 - Idiom meaning 'left like a yellow crane,' implying a sudden and mysterious departure.
  • 邻村某氏子 - Phrase meaning 'a son from a certain family in a neighboring village.'
  • 杭人寓居者 - Phrase meaning 'a person from Hangzhou who was living elsewhere.'
  • 得美妇 - Phrase meaning 'obtained a beautiful wife.'
  • 颇类狐女 - Phrase meaning 'quite similar to a fox girl.'
  • 隐刺刘姥 - Phrase meaning 'indirectly criticizing Old Lady Liu.'
  • 夫死再醮 - Phrase meaning 'after the husband's death, remarried.'
  • 悻悻出门 - Phrase meaning 'angrily leaving home.'
  • 视翁姑如寇仇 - Phrase meaning 'seeing one's in-laws as enemies.'
  • 终母之世 - Phrase meaning 'until the mother's lifetime.'
  • 供给勿衰 - Phrase meaning 'continued to provide support without decline.'
  • 樊英诚贤 - Phrase meaning 'Fan Ying was indeed virtuous.'
  • 惜其未明大节 - Phrase meaning 'it is regrettable that she did not understand the major principles.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 生亡 - Refers to the death of the husband, a significant event in traditional Chinese family life.
  • 披麻哭泣 - Traditional practice in Chinese funerary rites, indicating deep mourning.
  • 稽首 - Formal bow used in traditional Chinese rituals, showing respect and submission.
  • 新妇之责也 - Reflects the traditional expectation that a wife would take on the responsibility of caring for her in-laws after her husband's death.
  • 巨布囊 - Large cloth bag, often used in traditional Chinese settings for storing items.
  • 家稍裕 - Indicates a period of economic improvement, which is significant in the context of the story.
  • 黄鹤 - Mythical bird, often used in literature to symbolize something that has vanished mysteriously.
  • 邻村某氏子 - Refers to a person from a neighboring village, highlighting the local setting of the story.
  • 杭人寓居者 - Indicates that the person is from Hangzhou but currently living elsewhere, suggesting a connection to a different region.
  • 得美妇 - Suggests that the person obtained a beautiful wife, which is a common theme in traditional Chinese literature.
  • 颇类狐女 - Implies that the wife resembles a fox girl, a common motif in Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural or mysterious qualities.
  • 隐刺刘姥 - Suggests that the text is indirectly criticizing Old Lady Liu, possibly due to her behavior or actions.
  • 夫死再醮 - Refers to the practice of remarriage after the death of a spouse, which was a controversial topic in traditional Chinese society.
  • 悻悻出门 - Describes a person leaving angrily, indicating a negative emotional state.
  • 视翁姑如寇仇 - Describes a person treating their in-laws as enemies, which is a serious offense in traditional Chinese family values.
  • 终母之世 - Indicates a long period of time, emphasizing the duration of the support provided.
  • 供给勿衰 - Highlights the consistent and reliable support provided by the wife.
  • 樊英诚贤 - Acknowledges the virtue and goodness of Fan Ying.
  • 惜其未明大节 - Suggests a criticism of Fan Ying for not fully understanding the major principles or values.

Proper Nouns

  • 樊英 - The name of the daughter-in-law, Fan Ying.
  • 刘姥 - The name of the mother-in-law, Old Lady Liu.
  • 杭人 - Refers to people from Hangzhou.
  • 黄鹤 - Mythical bird, often used in literature to symbolize something that has vanished mysteriously.

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - A classical formula meaning 'The Tea-Drunk One says,' introducing the passage.
  • 明大節 - Means 'clarify the great principles' or 'emphasize the major virtues.'
  • 人且難之，况狐耶 - A rhetorical question meaning 'If humans find it difficult, how much more so foxes?'
  • 与其夫伉俪颇笃 - Means 'she was deeply devoted to her husband,' with '伉俪' referring to a married couple.
  • 夫死家贫，瘗诸荒冢 - Means 'after her husband died and they were poor, she buried him in a desolate grave.'
  • 越数载 - Means 'after several years.'
  • 长绳系马鞍，缚绳腰际 - Describes a method of dragging something by tying a rope to a horse's saddle and fastening it around the waist.
  • 曳之哭且行 - Means 'dragging it while crying and walking.'
  • 有所阻，就其处掘之 - Means 'if it encountered an obstacle, she would dig at that spot.'
  • 果得夫柩 - Means 'she indeed found her husband's coffin.'
  • 遂迁葬焉 - Means 'she then moved it to a new burial place.'
  • 可谓贤矣 - Means 'she can be called virtuous.'
  • 后晚年不贞 - Means 'later in her old age, she became unfaithful.'
  • 其主人有二童，先后私之 - Means 'her master had two young men who had sexual relations with her, one after the other.'
  • 容貌娴雅 - Means 'her appearance was elegant and refined.'
  • 夫妇甚和 - Means 'her husband and she were very harmonious.'
  • 夫死不嫁 - Means 'after her husband died, she did not remarry.'
  • 与某私，生子 - Means 'she had sexual relations with someone else and had a child.'
  • 伪为讨螟蛉者，以延夫嗣 - Means 'she pretended to adopt a child as her husband's heir.'
  • 深于情而越于礼矣 - Means 'she was deeply emotional but went beyond proper conduct.'
  • 以夫骨为重 - Means 'she valued her husband's remains.'
  • 必致之安土 - Means 'she insisted on burying him in a proper place.'
  • 知瘗夫为义，不知贻夫耻为大不义也 - Means 'she knew that burying her husband was right, but did not realize that bringing shame upon him was a great wrong.'
  • 无后为负夫 - Means 'not having descendants is a betrayal of one's husband.'
  • 犯淫为更负夫 - Means 'committing adultery is an even greater betrayal.'
  • 究其初心，未必真为夫嗣计 - Means 'examining her initial intentions, she may not have truly been considering her husband's lineage.'
  • 异姓螟蛉犹有乱宗之嫌 - Means 'adopting a child of a different surname is still suspicious of causing family disorder.'
  • 辱身乞种 - Means 'dishonoring oneself by seeking a child.'
  • 岂复有人理哉 - Means 'how could this be considered human behavior?'
  • 特淫焰炽盛，居然生子，又不忍割爱，遂日留而养之 - Means 'her lust was intense, and she actually had a child, and she could not bear to give it up, so she kept it and raised it.'
  • 机变之巧，无所用耻焉 - Means 'her cunning and resourcefulness made her ashamed of nothing.'
  • 女德无极 - Means 'female virtue has no limit.'
  • 第见其工貌胜人，即许为贤，奚可哉 - Means 'if one only sees that a woman is skilled and beautiful, and immediately considers her virtuous, how can that be right?'
  • 前妇愚蠢，此妇淫而多机警矣 - Means 'the former woman was foolish, while this one was promiscuous but clever.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醉茶子 - A fictional character or persona used to introduce the passage.
  • 狐 - Foxes are often used in Chinese literature to represent cunning or trickery.
  • 荒冢 - Refers to a desolate grave, often associated with poverty and neglect.
  • 长绳系马鞍 - A method of dragging something by tying a rope to a horse's saddle, reflecting practical solutions in ancient times.
  • 螟蛉 - In Chinese culture, '螟蛉' refers to a child adopted by a person of a different surname, often used metaphorically for illegitimate children.
  • 乱宗 - Refers to disrupting family lineage or causing family disorder.
  • 女德 - Refers to the virtues expected of women in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - A fictional character or persona used to introduce the passage.
  • 狐 - Foxes are often used in Chinese literature to represent cunning or trickery.
  • 荒冢 - Refers to a desolate grave, often associated with poverty and neglect.
  • 长绳系马鞍 - A method of dragging something by tying a rope to a horse's saddle.
  • 螟蛉 - In Chinese culture, '螟蛉' refers to a child adopted by a person of a different surname.
  • 乱宗 - Refers to disrupting family lineage or causing family disorder.
  • 女德 - Refers to the virtues expected of women in traditional Chinese society.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 役夫 - Refers to a common laborer or worker.
  • 相失在后 - Means 'lost and lagging behind,' indicating that the laborer became separated from the group.
  • 闬闳壮丽 - Describes the grandeur and beauty of the house, with '闬闳' referring to the gate or entrance.
  • 不敢遽入 - Means 'dared not enter immediately,' showing hesitation or caution.
  • 蹲踞门旁 - Means 'sat squatting by the door,' describing the laborer's posture.
  • 红妆颇艳 - Describes a woman dressed in red, appearing very beautiful.
  • 易耳 - A colloquial expression meaning 'easy' or 'no problem.'
  • 俟侬为尔煮茶 - Means 'wait for me to brew tea for you,' with '侬' being a term of endearment.
  • 玉碗金壶 - Describes the elegant and precious tea set.
  • 酌以佳茗 - Means 'poured fine tea,' indicating the quality of the tea.
  • 得茶已属望外 - Means 'receiving tea was already beyond expectations,' showing the laborer's surprise and gratitude.
  • 得毋饥乎 - Means 'are you not hungry?' with '得毋' being a polite way of asking.
  • 欢甚 - Means 'very happy,' expressing the laborer's joy.
  • 急应以唯唯 - Means 'quickly responded with assent,' indicating the laborer's eagerness to agree.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 辛卯夏 - Refers to the summer of the year 'Xin Mao' in the Chinese zodiac, which corresponds to a specific year in the sexagenary cycle.
  • 挖河之役 - Refers to a labor project involving digging a river, a common type of public works in ancient China.
  • 宁河兴工之所 - Refers to a specific location where the construction work was taking place, likely a river or canal project.
  • 女子红妆颇艳 - Describes a woman dressed in red, which was a traditional color for women in ancient China, symbolizing beauty and elegance.
  • 玉碗金壶 - Refers to the luxurious and precious materials used in the tea set, reflecting the high status of the household.

Proper Nouns

  • 辛卯 - A year in the Chinese zodiac, corresponding to a specific year in the sexagenary cycle.
  • 宁河 - A specific location where the construction work was taking place.
  • 玉碗金壶 - Refers to the luxurious and precious materials used in the tea set.
  • 佳茗 - Refers to the fine tea served to the laborer.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 女子乃招至寝室 - Formal expression meaning 'the woman then invited [the man] to her bedroom.'
  • 金玉锦锈，备极辉煌 - Describes opulence and splendor, literally 'gold, jade, brocade, and embroidery, all extremely brilliant.'
  • 所陈设之物，多不能指其名 - Means 'the objects displayed, most of which could not be named,' indicating their exotic or unfamiliar nature.
  • 坐立几不能自主 - Means 'he could hardly stand or sit on his own,' suggesting a state of awe or shock.
  • 罔不甘旨 - Means 'all were delicious,' with '罔不' emphasizing completeness.
  • 自惭形秽 - Classical idiom meaning 'ashamed of one's own appearance,' indicating a sense of inadequacy.
  • 踌躇不安 - Means 'hesitant and uneasy.'
  • 苟愿永好 - Means 'if you wish to stay forever,' with '苟' introducing a conditional.
  • 老于是乡 - Means 'age here in this place.'
  • 足尔一生温饱矣 - Means 'it will suffice for your entire life's comfort.'
  • 转自恐 - Means 'he began to feel fear,' with '转' indicating a shift in emotion.
  • 八日冻馁矣 - Means 'you would freeze and starve in eight days,' with '八日' emphasizing urgency.
  • 去则去耳，再来难矣 - Means 'if you leave, you leave; but returning will be difficult,' with '耳' indicating a finality and '难矣' emphasizing difficulty.
  • 禾黍油油 - Describes lush, thriving crops, with '油油' suggesting a smooth, glossy appearance.
  • 迥无屋宇 - Means 'completely without buildings,' with '迥' emphasizing the stark contrast.
  • 诧异而返 - Means 'they returned in astonishment,' with '诧异' indicating surprise and disbelief.
  • 未知是鬼是狐 - Means 'they did not know whether it was a ghost or a fox,' indicating uncertainty about the nature of the experience.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 女子 - Refers to a woman, possibly a supernatural or extraordinary figure given the context.
  • 寝室 - Refers to a bedroom, a private and intimate space.
  • 金玉锦锈 - Refers to precious materials, symbolizing wealth and luxury.
  • 役夫 - Refers to a laborer or servant, likely of low social status.
  • 饕餮 - A term for gluttony or excessive eating, often used in a negative sense.
  • 形秽 - Refers to physical or moral inferiority.
  • 温饱 - Refers to basic comfort and sustenance, a fundamental human need.
  • 冻馁 - Refers to freezing and starving, indicating extreme hardship.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 女子乃招至寝室，其中金玉锦锈，备极辉煌，所陈设之物，多不能指其名
  • 于是坐立几不能自主
  • 罔不甘旨，役夫大肆饕餮
  • 饭罢，女治枕栖，止之宿
  • 苟愿永好，老于是乡，足尔一生温饱矣
  • 转自恐，因云：‘家有老母妻子，须身自作苦，以供赡养。若久留于此，八日冻馁矣。请暂去复来。’
  • 去则去耳，再来难矣
  • 禾黍油油，迥无屋宇，诧异而返，未知是鬼是狐

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Classical formula for introducing a statement or opinion, similar to 'It is said' or 'One might say.'
  • 役夫蠢然一村汉耳 - Describes a laborer as a simple, uneducated peasant, with '蠢然' emphasizing his lack of sophistication.
  • 倚翠偎红 - Metaphor for enjoying the company of beautiful women, often associated with courtesans or concubines.
  • 数日艳福 - Refers to a brief period of indulgence in pleasure, particularly sexual pleasure.
  • 作得一日神仙，虽死无憾 - Saying meaning 'Even if one becomes a god for a day, dying without regret,' expressing the idea of seizing the moment.
  • 漫不加察 - Means 'carelessly' or 'without careful consideration.'
  • 冒然纳之 - Means 'rashly accept' or 'hastily take in.'
  • 陡然舍之 - Means 'suddenly discard' or 'abruptly abandon.'
  • 翩翩美少 - Describes a handsome, graceful young man.
  • 东床 - Euphemism for a prospective son-in-law, referring to the story of Wang Xizhi.
  • 采补之流 - Refers to practitioners of alchemy or Taoist practices aimed at immortality or longevity.
  • 数百年来，甘苦备尝 - Means 'over several centuries, experiencing both sweetness and bitterness,' indicating a long and varied life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 役夫 - Refers to a laborer or servant, often in a low social status.
  • 仙子 - Refers to a fairy or celestial being, often associated with beauty and grace.
  • 浙江某生 - Refers to an unnamed scholar from Zhejiang province, a region in eastern China known for its cultural and intellectual traditions.
  • 北闱乡试 - Refers to the provincial examination held in the northern capital (Beijing) during the imperial examination system.
  • 津门 - Refers to Tianjin, a major port city in northern China.
  • 兰若 - Refers to a Buddhist temple or hermitage, often associated with seclusion and meditation.
  • 僧 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
  • 斗室 - Refers to a small room, often used to describe a humble living space.
  • 承尘 - Refers to the ceiling, often associated with the idea of the heavens or the upper world.
  • 炼形拜月，吐纳采补 - Refers to Taoist practices involving physical transformation, moon worship, and breathing exercises aimed at absorbing energy.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - A classical figure or persona, possibly a fictional or allegorical character.
  • 役夫 - A laborer or servant.
  • 仙子 - A fairy or celestial being.
  • 浙江某生 - An unnamed scholar from Zhejiang province.
  • 北闱 - The northern capital (Beijing).
  • 津门 - Tianjin, a major port city in northern China.
  • 兰若 - A Buddhist temple or hermitage.
  • 僧 - A Buddhist monk.
  • 斗室 - A small room.
  • 承尘 - The ceiling.
  • 狐 - A fox, often associated with spirits or magical beings in Chinese folklore.
  • 炼形拜月，吐纳采补 - Taoist practices involving physical transformation, moon worship, and breathing exercises.

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至今日 - Temporal expression meaning 'up to this day' or 'until now.'
  • 支体官骸 - Literally 'limbs and official bones,' a metaphorical expression referring to the physical body.
  • 大丹将成 - Daoist term meaning 'the great elixir is about to be completed,' referring to the achievement of immortality or spiritual perfection.
  • 顿遭雷劫 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'suddenly face a thunderbolt calamity,' referring to sudden misfortune or disaster.
  • 夙昔功苦 - Literally 'past efforts and hardships,' referring to previous struggles and achievements.
  • 瓦解 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'disintegrate' or 'collapse.'
  • 尽归灰烬 - Literally 'all return to ashes,' meaning 'completely destroyed.'
  • 殊觉可惜 - Literally 'indeed very regrettable,' expressing deep regret.
  • 思量至此 - Literally 'thinking to this point,' meaning 'considering this far.'
  • 肝为之摧 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the liver is crushed,' indicating extreme emotional distress.
  • 心为之酸 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the heart is pained,' indicating emotional pain.
  • 潸焉出涕 - Literally 'tears flow involuntarily,' meaning 'tears stream down.'
  • 几不知泪之所自来也 - Literally 'almost not knowing where the tears come from,' meaning 'not knowing why one is crying.'
  • 转思幸免雷殛 - Literally 'turning to think of being fortunate to avoid the thunderbolt,' meaning 'considering oneself fortunate to have avoided disaster.'
  • 从此升碧霄 - Literally 'from now on ascend to the blue sky,' meaning 'ascend to heaven.'
  • 登紫府 - Literally 'enter the purple palace,' a metaphorical expression referring to reaching a high spiritual realm.
  • 优游自得 - Literally 'leisurely and content,' meaning 'living in ease and satisfaction.'
  • 与黄石、赤松辈共朝夕 - Literally 'with Yellow Stone and Red Pine and others share daily life,' referring to living with famous immortals.
  • 将入王母之宫 - Literally 'about to enter the Queen Mother's palace,' referring to entering the heavenly abode of the Queen Mother of the West.
  • 而听董双成之雅乐 - Literally 'and listen to the elegant music of Dong Shuangcheng,' referring to enjoying heavenly music.
  • 许飞琼之妙音 - Literally 'and enjoy the exquisite sounds of Xu Feiqiong,' referring to enjoying heavenly music.
  • 餐沆瀣 - Literally 'eat the mist and dew,' meaning 'eat heavenly food.'
  • 咽琼浆 - Literally 'swallow the jade liquor,' meaning 'drink heavenly nectar.'
  • 食仙桃 - Literally 'eat the immortal peach,' referring to eating the fruit of immortality.
  • 长生不老 - Literally 'live forever without aging,' meaning 'immortality.'
  • 俯视尘世之冠冕 - Literally 'look down on the crowns of the world,' meaning 'look down on the worldly elite.'
  • 真若泥涂富贵 - Literally 'truly like mud and mire wealth,' meaning 'truly like mud and mire.'
  • 直如幻梦 - Literally 'just like a dream,' meaning 'just like a dream.'
  • 不亦乐乎 - Classical expression meaning 'isn't that joyful?'
  • 不禁为之狂笑 - Literally 'cannot help but laugh wildly,' meaning 'cannot help but laugh uncontrollably.'
  • 尔休矣 - Literally 'you stop,' meaning 'you stop.'
  • 凡修道之人 - Literally 'all those who practice the Dao,' referring to practitioners of Daoism.
  • 心宜淡定 - Literally 'the mind should be calm,' meaning 'the mind should be calm.'
  • 尔私欲撞扰 - Literally 'your selfish desires disturb,' meaning 'your selfish desires disturb.'
  • 倾刻万念 - Literally 'in a moment, countless thoughts,' meaning 'in a moment, countless thoughts.'
  • 甚非法也 - Literally 'very unrighteous,' meaning 'very wrong.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大丹 - Refers to the great elixir in Daoist alchemy, which is believed to grant immortality.
  • 雷劫 - Metaphorical expression in Daoism referring to sudden misfortune or disaster.
  • 夙昔功苦 - Refers to past efforts and hardships, often used in the context of spiritual practice.
  • 黄 - Refers to Yellow Stone, a famous immortal in Chinese mythology.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 刺剌强辩 - Compound term meaning 'to argue persistently and annoyingly.' *刺剌* suggests a continuous, nagging sound, and *强辩* means 'to argue stubbornly' or 'to insist on one's point despite evidence to the contrary.'
  • 悲喜交集 - Classical idiom meaning 'a mixture of sorrow and joy,' or 'to feel both sadness and happiness at the same time.'
  • 絮絮聒聒 - Colloquial expression meaning 'to talk on and on annoyingly' or 'to chatter incessantly.'
  • 扰我清眠 - Phrase meaning 'to disturb my peaceful sleep,' combining *扰* (to disturb) and *清眠* (clear or peaceful sleep).
  • 觅天师符印至 - Phrase that literally means 'to go and fetch the talisman and seal of the Heavenly Master.' *天师* refers to the Taoist deity or a Taoist priest, particularly associated with the Zhang family (e.g., Zhang Daoling), and *符印* are magical talismans and seals used in Taoist rituals.
  • 暗喜狐之畏己也 - Phrase meaning 'secretly delighted that the fox fears him.' *暗喜* means 'secretly delighted,' and *畏己* means 'fears him.'
  • 大呼曰 - Formulaic expression meaning 'shouted loudly, saying...'
  • 某人 - Generic term for 'someone' or 'a certain person,' often used in narratives to refer to a character without naming them.
  • 责我而不自责 - 'To blame me but not oneself,' indicating hypocrisy or self-righteousness.
  • 前科乡试出场后 - Refers to 'after leaving the examination hall following the previous county-level examination (乡试).' *前科* means 'previous attempt' or 'previous examination.'
  • 在京寓候榜 - 'To wait in the capital's residence for the results of the examination.'
  • 作得意想 - 'To imagine oneself as successful' or 'to feel elated with the thought of success.'
  • 手舞足蹈 - Classical idiom meaning 'to dance with joy' or 'to be so elated that one is literally dancing.'
  • 秋折桂 - Metaphor for passing the imperial examination, as *折桂* (plucking the osmanthus) was a poetic way of saying 'to win the top honor.'
  • 明春攀杏 - Another metaphor for success, as *攀杏* refers to climbing the apricot tree, a symbol of passing the examination and achieving honor.
  • 高车驷马 - Phrase meaning 'a high carriage pulled by four horses,' symbolizing wealth and status.
  • 华丽其衣服 - 'To dress in luxurious clothing.'
  • 夸耀夫乡里 - 'To boast among the village or hometown.'
  • 高步青云 - 'To ascend to the clouds,' a metaphor for rising to high office or status.
  • 俯视一切 - 'To look down on everything,' indicating superiority.
  • 乐何如之 - 'How happy is this?' or 'How delightful is this?'
  • 拊掌大笑 - 'To clap one's hands and laugh loudly,' indicating great joy or amusement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浙人 - Refers to someone from Zhejiang province, indicating a regional identity.
  • 乡试 - A county-level examination in the imperial examination system, part of the *shi* (literary) examination process.
  • 天师符印 - Refers to Taoist magical items used in rituals to exorcise evil spirits or impose supernatural authority. *天师* (Heavenly Master) is a title for a Taoist priest or deity, most famously associated with Zhang Daoling, the founder of the Way of the Celestial Masters.
  • 高车驷马 - A metaphor for wealth and status in ancient China, as high-ranking officials were often depicted in such grand vehicles.
  • 折桂 - A classical metaphor for passing an important examination or achieving success, derived from the legend that the osmanthus tree is a symbol of academic success.
  • 攀杏 - Another metaphor for success, as the apricot tree was also associated with scholarly achievement and honor.
  • 乡里 - Refers to one’s hometown or village, a common term in classical Chinese literature.
  • 青云 - Refers to the clouds, symbolizing high office or lofty status in classical Chinese metaphors.
  • 拊掌大笑 - A common expression in classical literature to depict joy, often used to describe the reaction of characters to a successful outcome or a humorous situation.

Proper Nouns

  • 浙人 - A geographical term referring to people from Zhejiang province.
